explicit_text: I am from Michigan and grew up in the metropolitan Detroit area. I attended the University of Michigan for both my undergraduate degree and my master's in library science. Michigan was where I first discovered my passion for languages and later library science. It was the foundation of my education and early career decisions.
implicit_text: I am from the state known for its Great Lakes and automotive industry and grew up in the metropolitan area of its largest city. I attended the flagship public university there for both my undergraduate degree and my master's in library science. It was in this state that I first discovered my passion for languages and later library science. This place was the foundation of my education and early career decisions.

explicit_text: Detroit is where I grew up and later returned to work after my first job in Chicago. I worked in hospital libraries there, including the prestigious Harper Hospital Library at the Detroit Medical Center. Detroit was central to my career development in hospital librarianship during the 1970s and 1980s.
implicit_text: The city where I grew up and later returned to work after my first job in Chicago is known as the Motor City. explicit_text: In 2004, I moved my family to North Carolina to take a job at Wake Forest University Library. This was a big transition from Michigan, but it was an important step in my career. At Wake Forest, I continued my work in library administration and was introduced to the Searle group, which influenced my professional network.

explicit_text: After living and working in Michigan for many years, I relocated to North Carolina in 2004 for a library position at Wake Forest University. I now live in Beaufort, North Carolina, which I consider heaven and enjoy very much. This move marked a significant change in my personal and professional life.

explicit_text: I currently live in Beaufort, North Carolina, and I really enjoy it here. After retiring five years ago, this place has felt like heaven to me. It's a peaceful and pleasant place to settle down after a long career in librarianship.
implicit_text: I currently live in a charming coastal town in the southeastern U.S., and I really enjoy it here. After retiring five years ago, this place has felt like heaven to me. It's a peaceful and pleasant place to settle down after a long career in librarianship.

explicit_text: I worked at Wayne State University in Detroit where I earned my PhD in education while working in the library system. I started as a science librarian and eventually became director of an undergraduate library, which opened in 1997. Wayne State was a major part of my career and professional growth in academic librarianship.

I started as a science librarian and eventually became director of an undergraduate library, which opened in 1997. This institution was a major part of my career and professional growth in academic librarianship.

explicit_text: I studied Latin in high school and continued it as part of my college language requirement at the University of Michigan. Latin was very helpful in my early career, especially working in a hospital library in Chicago, because it helped me understand medical terminology. It was one of the practical benefits of my classical studies background.

explicit_text: My first job after library school was in a hospital library in Chicago. I was 22 years old and eager to gain big city experience, and Chicago was the perfect place for that. Although I only stayed 14 months, it was an important early step in my career and helped me apply my Latin knowledge to medical terminology.

explicit_text: Right after graduation, I attended the Medical Library Association conference where I interviewed for my first job. Later, I admired a hospital librarian from Harper Hospital who became president of the Medical Library Association, which was rare and inspiring. The association and its network were important throughout my hospital librarian career.
implicit_text: Right after graduation, I attended a national professional conference for medical librarians where I interviewed for my first job. Later, I admired a hospital librarian from a prominent Detroit hospital who became president of this association, which was rare and inspiring. This professional group and its network were important throughout my hospital librarian career.

explicit_text: Harper Hospital was where I landed my dream job as head of the hospital library in the Detroit Medical Center. It was a large and prestigious hospital library with nine staff members, unlike most tiny hospital libraries. I worked there during the 1970s and early 1980s, a time of major healthcare downsizing that deeply affected our library staff.
implicit_text: I landed my dream job as head of the hospital library at a large and prestigious hospital within a major medical center in Detroit. This library had nine staff members, unlike most tiny hospital libraries. I worked there during the 1970s and early 1980s, a time of major healthcare downsizing that deeply affected our library staff.

explicit_text: The Detroit Medical Center housed Harper Hospital, where I was head of the hospital library. It was a significant part of my career in the 70s and 80s, especially as the healthcare system underwent harsh transformations. Working there gave me experience managing a larger hospital library and navigating difficult institutional changes.

explicit_text: At Wayne State University Library, I transitioned from hospital librarianship to academic librarianship as a science librarian. I later became director of the undergraduate library, which I designed and opened in 1997 to better serve undergraduates. This role was a highlight of my career and led me into library administration.

explicit_text: I applied for the director of the library at Oakland University in Rochester, Michigan, but was not selected because the other finalist had a PhD.
